methods.name: get_node
methods.doc:
Return nodes that match either node name or component name, optionally filtered by edge connection type.

methods.name: walk_nodes
methods.doc:
Generator that yields (node, depth, parent) for every node in the map.

Args:
    start_node: Starting node for the walk. If None, uses self.top_node.
    max_depth: Maximum depth to traverse. If None, traverses all levels.
    search_type: Type of search - "dfs" (depth-first) or "bfs" (breadth-first). Default is "dfs".

methods.name: print_node_walk
methods.doc:
Print a depth-first or breadth-first walk of the PM map.

Args:
    start_node: Starting node for the walk. If None, uses self.top_node.
    max_depth: Maximum depth to traverse. If None, traverses all levels.
    search_type: Type of search - "dfs" (depth-first) or "bfs" (breadth-first). Default is "dfs".

methods.name: display_map
methods.doc:
Draw the PM mapping as a top-down hierarchical tree and output as SVG.
No external plotting library required — pure SVG generation.

Layout guarantees:
  - Root at the top; leaves at the bottom.
  - Each subtree owns an exclusive horizontal strip proportional to its
    leaf count, so sibling subtrees never share x-space and edges
    between different parent-child pairs never cross.
  - Multi-edges between the same pair are offset horizontally so port
    labels never stack on top of each other.
  - Node rectangles are sized to contain wrapped text; all nodes at the
    same depth share the same height so the grid stays uniform.
  - LEAF_SLOT_W > NODE_W guarantees leaf-level nodes never overlap.
  - Nodes with ≥2 outgoing connection types show a color-coded sub-block
    row at the bottom for each type; edges connect from those sub-blocks.

Args:
    start_node: Optional node to focus the display. When provided,
                the rendered view includes:
                1) all descendants of start_node,
                2) all ancestors of start_node up to layout roots.
                When None, renders the entire map.
    save_to_file: File path to save the SVG (e.g. "map.svg").
                  When None the SVG is written to a temp file and
                  opened in the default web browser.

functions.name: resolve_component_from_name
functions.doc:
Resolve a RegisterComponent from a string representation, if possible.

The string can be in the format of a full PythonSV path (e.g. "socket0.imh0.isa.isa_mio_1") or just the component name (e.g. "isa_mio_1"). The function will attempt to resolve the component using PythonSV if the full path is provided, and will fall back to searching for a component with the matching name if only the component name is provided.

Args:
    component_str: String representation of the component to resolve.
Returns:
    The resolved RegisterComponent object if found, otherwise None.

A series of utility to convert bit strings into floating point, integer, etc...

classes.name: convert
classes.doc: Various functions to do binary/hex/floating point conversions and manipulations

methods.name: bin
methods.doc:
Return a binary representation of a number

Args:
    value (str): pass the value to be converted to binary.  Can handle decimal or hexidecimal (0xf)

methods.name: getbits
methods.doc:
Return a subset of a binary string given separate upper and lower bit positions.

Args:
    value (int): pass the value to be examined.
    upperbit (int): the upper bit position.
    lowerbit (int): the lower bit position.

methods.name: getBits2
methods.doc:
Return a subset of a binary string given a range.

Args:
    value (int): pass the value to be examined.
    rangeStr (str): the bit range to extract: examples "17:6" or "[17:6]".

methods.name: setbits
methods.doc:
Sets bits specified in the value to bitvalue and returns new value.

Args:
    value (int): pass the value to be examined.
    upperbit (int): MSB bit position.
    lowerbit (int): MSB bit position.
    bitvalue (int): pass the bitvalue to be set into the value.

methods.name: setBits2
methods.doc:
Sets bits specified in the value to bitvalue and returns new value.

Args:
    value (int): pass the value to be examined
    bitvalue (int): pass the bitvalue to be set into the value
    rangeStr (str): the bit range to extract: examples "17:6" or "[17:6]"

methods.name: float2bin
methods.doc:
Convert a number into binary format using the provided format definition.

Args:
    data (int/float/str): pass the value to be converted into a binary format.
    fmt (str): Fixed floating point format, 'float', 'double' and 'int32' (signed int32)

Fixed floating point syntax:
    S16.2.13
    ^ ^   ^
    | |   +- exponent
    | +----- significand size
    | +----- size
    +------- Signed / Unsigned

methods.name: bin2float
methods.doc:
Convert binary number into float or signed int32 format using the provided format definition

Args:
    binary (str): pass the value to be converted from a float or signed int32 format
    fmt (str): Fixed floating point format, 'float', 'double' and 'int32' (signed int32)

Format Syntax::

    S16.2.13
    ^ ^   ^
    | |   +- exponent
    | +----- significand size
    | +----- size
    +------- Signed / Unsigned

classes.name: DeltaTracker
classes.doc:
Tracks the change (delta) in counter values over time, handling wrap-around for counters that reset after reaching their maximum value.
Stores previous and current values for each counter and computes the difference between samples.
Useful for counters that increment over time and may wrap around.

classes.name: ActualTracker
classes.doc:
Returns the actual (current) value of each counter, scaled as needed.
Does not compute deltas; simply reports the latest sampled value for each counter.
Useful when you want to observe the absolute value of counters rather than their change.

classes.name: BwTracker
classes.doc:
Tracks the bandwidth (rate of change) for each counter by dividing the delta by the elapsed time between samples.
Stores timestamps for each sample to accurately compute the rate.
Useful for monitoring bandwidth or throughput metrics where the rate of change is important.

functions.name: set_ufs_heuristics_enable_mask
functions.doc:
Present-in: DMR/release-drops/primecode-2025ww34-4350

Set the dfx_ufs_heuristics_enable_mask for UFS heuristics.

Args:
    value (int, optional): Value to set the mask to. If None, enables all bits.
    sockets (list, optional): List of socket indices to set. If None, sets all sockets.
    imhs (list, optional): List of IMH indices to set. If None, sets all IMHs.

Example:
    # Enable all features on all sockets/IMHs
    set_ufs_heuristics_enable_mask()

    # Disable all features on socket 0, IMH 1
    set_ufs_heuristics_enable_mask(0, sockets=[0], imhs=[1])

    # Enable only mem_bound_check (bit 0) and io_traffic_check (bit 5) on socket 1
    mask = (1 << 0) | (1 << 5)
    set_ufs_heuristics_enable_mask(mask, sockets=[1])

enum class ufs_heuristics_feature_bits : uint8_t
{
    mem_bound_check         = 0,
    mem_d2d_check           = 1,
    mem_ring_bw_check       = 2,
    mem_mc_traffic_check    = 3,
    mesh_boost_check        = 4,
    io_traffic_check        = 5,
    elc_mode_check          = 6,
    perf_mode_check         = 7,
    NUM_FEATURES            = 8
};

classes.name: FlexconCallBack
classes.doc:
Description:
    Base class for FlexconCallBack. Not meant for plugin usage.

Usage:
    A "FlexconCallback" is a function pointer that flexcon will "call back" into the plugin.
    Each callback's purpose is to validate that a silicon feature is correctly configured.
    Confirming how any given feature is expected to be configured may require reading BIOS knobs and/or fuses (both of which can be accomplished through functions in this file), then confirming that the required register configuration is correct.
    Example: QPI L0s --> If (BiosKnob.L0sEnble == "Enabled" or "Auto") AND (Fuse.qpiL0s_disable == 0), then confirm all L0s programming. Else: Confirm L0s disabled.

Flexcon Naming Convention:
   From the main flexcon application, each callback is referred to by the following schema: <module>.<tag>.<function/name>
   Flexcon provides the ability to disable any group of checks (a single function, an entire tag, or an entire module).
   The intention of the 3-level naming-schema is to provide developers with a dynamic naming schema for their callbacks so a broken check does not result in an entire module or tag getting disabled by Execution.

methods.name: __init__
methods.doc:
Description:
    Flexcon Callback Constructor.

Parameters:
    handle              Function Pointer which is a flexcon callback
    severity      Severity of CallBack ("ENABLED", "WARNING", "INFO", "DISABLED").
                      Enabled must pass or result in flexcon failure.
                      Warning can fail but doesn't result in a flexcon failure.
                      Info can fail but doesn't result in a flexcon failure and not counted.
                      Disabled are not called.
    tag            Flexcon Callback Tag (see Naming Convention).
    name                  Flexcon Callback Name (see Naming Convention).
    owner                Flexcon Callback Owner. This is the person who should be contacted if ths check fails.
    modname        Override name for the flexcon module
    cbargs              Arguments which should be passed into callback
    cbkwargs      Keyword arguments which should be passed into the callback

methods.name: execute
methods.doc:
"
Description:
    Execute Callback with all parameters (args & kwargs)

classes.name: FlexconCallBackList
classes.doc:
Description:
    Flexcon Callback List Class. This class's purpose is to simplify how flexconCallbacks are created by allowing the user to globally set some of the parameters.

Example:
    #assume this code is in flexconExample.py
    list = flexconCallBackList()                      # list constructor
    list.setOwner("Dr. Frankenstein")                # All callbacks registered after this point will have this owner
    list.setTag("Foo")                                              # Set Tag for all callbacks
    list.addCallBack(cbTest,args=[socket0])   # Appends: flexconExample.Foo.cbTest (which will be called with socket0 as a parameter)
    list.addVerify("socket0.uncore0.rid",0)   # Appends: flexconExample.Foo.Verify

methods.name: __init__
methods.doc:
Description:
    FlexconCallBack Constructor.

Parameters:
    None

methods.name: setTag
methods.doc:
Description:
    Set the FlexconList's Tag for all callbacks added to list.

Parameter:
    tag         FlexconTag (default="default")

methods.name: setModule
methods.doc:
Description:
    Set the FlexconList's Module name for all callbacks added afterwards.

Parameter:
    module        Flexcon Module (default="flexcon***") (*** is the actual module name)

methods.name: setSeverity
methods.doc:
Description:
    Set the FlexconList's Severity for all callbacks added to list.

Parameter:
    severity   Flexcon Severity (default:"ENABLED")
        "ENABLED" / "WARNING" / "INFO" / "DISABLED"

methods.name: setHandle
methods.doc:
Description:
    Set the FlexconList's Handle for all callbacks added to list.

Parameter:
    handle       Handle to function that you want to use to register all future callbacks.

methods.name: setName
methods.doc:
Description:
    Set the FlexconList's Name (replacement for function) for all callbacks added to list.

Parameter:
    name           Flexcon Name for callback (default: whatever the function's name is)

methods.name: setOwner
methods.doc:
Description:
    Set the FlexconList's Owner for all callbacks added to list.

Parameter:
    owner         Flexcon Owner of all callbacks added to list

methods.name: setFlags
methods.doc:
Description:
    Set the FlexconList's Owner for all callbacks added to list.

Parameter:
    flags       Flexcon flags (Value of [2:0]=0x0 means no conditional on pre, normal, post
                0x0 =  CB_DEFAULT               - Default:      Run Pre, Norm/Default, Post in order. No qualifiers to remove.
                0x1 =  CB_NORM_REQ         - Normal Callbacks.
                0x2 =  CB_PRE_REQ               - Callback will be done before Normal Callbacks (higher priority)
                0x4 =  CB_POST_REQ         - Callback will be done after Normal Callback   (lower priority)
                0x8 =  CB_UNLOCK_REQ     - Callback requires Unlock (ITP) or patch2/3 (SVOS). Otherwise, skip.
                0x10 = CB_HALT_REQ         - Callback requires ITP halt if run via ITP.
                0x20 = CB_STUB_EXEMPT   - Callback requires ITP halt if run via ITP.
                0x40 = CB_ITP_EXEMPT     - Callback requires ITP halt if run via ITP.
                0x80 = CB_SVOS_EXEMPT   - Callback requires ITP halt if run via ITP.

methods.name: addCallBack
methods.doc:
Description:
    Add a flexcon Callback to the FlexconCallBackList.

Parameters:
    handle              Function Pointer which is a flexcon callback
    severity      Severity of CallBack ("ENABLED", "WARNING", "INFO", "DISABLED").
                      Enabled must pass or result in flexcon failure.
                      Warning can fail but don't result in a flexcon failure.
                      Disabled are not called.
    tag            Flexcon Callback Tag (see Naming Convention).
    name                  Flexcon Callback Name (see Naming Convention).
    owner                Flexcon Callback Owner. This is the person who should be contacted if ths check fails.
    flags                Flexcon flags
                    0x0 =  CB_DEFAULT           - Default:      Run Pre, Norm/Default, Post in order. No qualifiers to remove.
                    0x1 =  CB_NORM_REQ     - Normal Callbacks.
                    0x2 =  CB_PRE_REQ           - Callback before Normal Callbacks (higher priority)
                    0x4 =  CB_POST_REQ     - Callback after Normal Callbacks   (lower priority)
                    0x8 =  CB_UNLOCK_REQ         - Callback requires Unlock (ITP) or patch2/3 (SVOS). Otherwise, skip.
                    0x10 = CB_HALT_REQ     - Callback requires ITP halt if run via ITP.
                    0x20 = CB_STUB_EXEMPT       - Callback requires ITP halt if run via ITP.
                    0x40 = CB_ITP_EXEMPT         - Callback requires ITP halt if run via ITP.
                    0x80 = CB_SVOS_EXEMPT       - Callback requires ITP halt if run via ITP.
    cbargs              Arguments which should be passed into callback
    cbkwargs      Keyword arguments which should be passed into the callback

methods.name: addVerify
methods.doc:
Description:
   Add a simple register verify to the flexconCallback List. Device is read and compared against op_value

Parameters:
   device               Component object which Verify is to take place on
                 <component object> or string ("socket0.uncore0.<reg>.<field>")
   op_value       Numerical Value or string with logical operator (">=3") which to compare device's value against
   pretty_name   Pretty Name for check (for easy printing)
   owner                 Owner of Flexcon check
   cbname               Name of Callback (replaces "Verify")
   cbtag                 Tag for Callback

   cbflags         See flexconUtils.CBFLAGS documentation for available flags

classes.name: CBFLAGS
classes.doc:
Description:
    Callback flags container for supported flags

Available Flags:

    CB_DEFAULT                Default Operation is to run Pre, Norm, Post.
    CB_NORM_REQ              Normal Callback in reference to order.
    CB_PRE_REQ                Callback will be done before Normal Callbacks (higher priority)
    CB_POST_REQ              Callback will be done after Normal Callback   (lower priority)
    CB_UNLOCK_REQ      Callback requires Unlock (ITP) and patch2/3 (SVOS). Otherwise, skip.
    CB_HALT_REQ              Callback requires ITP halt if run via ITP.
    CB_STUB_EXEMPT    Callback is skipped if run on stub
    CB_ITP_EXEMPT      Callback is skipped if run through ITP
    CB_HOST_EXEMPT     Callback is skipped if run through ITP
    CB_SVOS_EXEMPT    Callback is skipped if run in SVOS
    CB_STUB_REQ              Callback requires to be run on stub
    CB_ITP_REQ                Callback requires to be run through ITP
    CB_HOST_REQ              Callback requires to be run through ITP (Same as above, preserved for backwards compatibility)
    CB_SVOS_REQ              Callback requires to be run through SVOS
    CB_INT_BIOS_REQ  Callback requires INT BIOS type
    CB_CRB_BIOS_REQ  Callback requires CRB BIOS type

Description:

                   Supports bit ranges as such:
                    string ("socket0.uncore0.<reg>.<field>.[bit]")
                    string ("socket0.uncore0.<reg>.<field>.[start:stop]") where start can equal stop
    op_value                 Numerical Value or string with logical operator (">=3") which to compare device's value against
    pretty_name       Pretty Name for check (for easy printing)
    owner                   Owner of Flexcon check

functions.name: VerifyWrite
functions.doc:
Description:
    VerifyWrite a Device (either string or componentValue) is read and compared against op_value. This is primarily meant to be used by the

functions.name: expandDeviceCC
functions.doc:
Description:
    String Device expander. Expands regular expressions by looking at all children attributes & matching until all are found

Parameters:
    device_str        String of device to expand
    pdevice          Parent Device to recursively search through
    exact              Whether to do EXACT matching or substring matching (True/False)

Usage:
    expandDevice("socket0.uncore0.rid*",pdevice=sv)
    --> ["rid","rid_cr","rid_d10f0","rid_d10f1",...]

Return:
    List of strings of all matched devices starting at parent

functions.name: p2a_mailbox
functions.doc:
P-code to A-Code mailbox function.

Args:
    sktNum (int): socket integer
    coreNum (int): core number
    command (int | str): ACODE_PCODE_MAILBOX_CMD value or name string
    group (int): param1
    subGroup (int): param2
    data0 (int | None, optional): data0
    data1 (int | None, optional): data1
    log (slt.EasyLogger, optional): Logger object. Defaults to _LOG.

Returns:
    tuple[tuple[int, str], int | None]: ((return_code, decode), return_data)

functions.name: a2p_mailbox
functions.doc:
A-Code to P-Code mailbox function.

Args:
    sktNum (int): socket integer
    coreNum (int): core number
    command (int | str): ACODE_PCODE_MAILBOX_CMD value or name string
    group (int): param1
    subGroup (int): param2
    data0 (int | None, optional): data0
    data1 (int | None, optional): data1
    log (slt.EasyLogger, optional): Logger object. Defaults to _LOG.

Returns:
    tuple[tuple[int, str], int | None, int | None]: ((return_code, decode), return_data_0, return_data_1)

Raises:
    RuntimeError: If socket number is invalid or if core number is larger than number of cores or if device is unknown

Periodically read IMON power for a VR

Args:
    sktNum (int): Which socket to poll IMON data frame
    vrs (int/list): a list of VR ids
    src (str): read imon or read SVID

module_doc:
Team: PM

Sub-Team:  PM Idle (c-states), PM Active (p-states)

Owner:  Perez Macias, Lizette 

email:  lizette.perez.macias@intel.com

Intent of the script: PEGA (Power Event Generation Arch.) is a harasser that gets generated from pcode. It
supports both p-states and c-states in parallel. A c-state command will be requested over the
    io_tap_mailbox_data/interface as its own special command. The p-state requests just use the GVFSM.
    The main commands to use in this script are pega_pstate and pega_cstate

Input arguments/options for the script: 
    "-d", "--domainDict" => Used to pass in the target value dictionary. Ex: "{'C3':[1,2,3],'C1':[0,4]}" or "{2.6:'all'}" note that "all" doesn't include uncore, default=False
    "--dn","--dieName" => Identify dieName eg. io0, io1, ios, compute0, etc..., default='all'
    "-u", "--clrgv" => CLR_GV target value when issuing pega p-states. Random is a negative integer, default=0
    "--ufs", dest="clrgv" => CLR_GV target value when issuing pega p-states. Random is a negative integer , default=0
    "-t", "--cmdtype" => The type of pega command you want to invoke, 
this only needs to be specified when using the valDict. (ex: 'pstate' or 'cstate'),default="False"
    "-c", "--cstate" => Value of c-states that are wanted for this test. Ex: c1, c3, c6, rand, default=False
    "-k", "--pkgc" => Specifies which pkgc you would like to achieve, default=False
    "-g", "--pstate" => Value of p-states that are wanted for this test. Ex: 2.0, 0xc, default=False
    "-a", "--act2ms" => Timer value in ms for action2. A negative value is random, default=0.0
    "-r", "--rearmms" => Timer value in ms for rearm. A negative value is random, default=0.0
    "-x", "--stop" => Used if you want a stop a pega command. Also, need to set --cmdtype option, default=False
    "-s", "--skt", => Used to specify which socket to target with pega. Default is 'all', default='all' 
    "--unit" => Use this for specifying a rearm unit with random timer values, default=0, default=0
    "-l", "--loglevel" => The log level to be used. Do not put quotes around the argument, default=False
    "-C", "--consolelevel" => The console level to be used. Do not put quotes around the argument, default=False
    "-m", "--randmask" => Set if random mask is needed for domains, default=False
    "-z", "--clrgvSktsDict" => Used to pass in the target value dictionary. Ex: "{0:'rand'',1:'max',2:'min'}", default=False
    "-D", "--delay" => Use this to sleep pega for a determined amount of time, default=0

Script usage example:  
Usage as standalone: runPmx.py -p pstates_core --pstate rand -r 100

functions.name: pegaPstate
functions.doc:
Input:
    sktNum (int):           This is the socket number that you want to target with PEGA Dfx
    dieName (str):  This is the die name or 'all' that you want to tagret with PEGA Dfx
    domainRatioDict (dict): This is a dictionary of target values with core number pairs. The key in the dictionary
                            is the requsted value and the values for each key are the list of cores(int).
                            Ex: {0xe:[1,2], 2.6:[0,3]}
    clrgv (hex/int):        This is the value for PEGA to target the CLR_GV with.
    rearmTimems (float):    This is the time in milliseconds that the pega overide will wait to take place.
                            If 0  then this action will only be issued once.
                            If -1 then will use random units and timers
    act2Timems (float):     This is the time in milliseconds that the pega overide will wait to release.
                            If 0 then action2 is never issued.
                            If -1, then action2 is random units and timers
    randMask (bool):        This is a boolean value to state where a random target mask will be used with pega.
    randUnit (int):         Select a time unit if using random timers

Output:
    Writes to command registers (io_io_tap_mailbox_data and io_io_tap_mailbox_interface)

functions.name: pegaCstate
functions.doc:
Args:
    sktNum (int): This is the socket number or 'all' that you want to target with PEGA Dfx
    dieName (str):  This is the die name or 'all' that you want to tagret with PEGA Dfx
    domainDict (dict of list of hex/int): This dictionary contains the list of values to inject for pega for each of the selected power domains.
                            If random is wanted then use a value of -1.
                            Ex: {'C3':[1,2,3],'C6':[0,4]} if int type is used instead of list then that will be used as a mask
                            {'r':[1,2,3]
    rearmTimems (float):      This is the time in milliseconds that the pega overide will take place. If a value of 0 is pafosses
                            then this action will only be issued once.
    act2Timems (float):              This is the time in milliseconds that the pega overide will wait to release If a value of 0 is passes
                            then action2 is never issued.
    randMask(Bool): This is a boolean value to state where a random target mask will be used with pega.
    randUnit(int): Unit select to use if using random rearm or action2 timer values

Output:
    Writes to command registers (io_io_tap_mailbox_data and io_io_tap_mailbox_interface)

methods.name: get_gv_enable
methods.doc:
Command:
    get_gv_enable(return_option=False)

Details:
    Returns True if gv3 is enabled, else False
    Sources:
    - pcudata
    - misc_config

Inputs:
    return_option  - return table (default = False)

Outputs:
    header, table

EX:
    >>> get_gv_enable()  # will print all sources
    >>> get_gv_enable(return_opt=True)  # will print all sources and return the table

methods.doc:

Details:

Inputs:

                  default: max posible value for each bucket hence disabling this imput

Outputs:
    None.

EX:
    >>> set_ia_turbo_ratio_limits_primary(trl_list=[35,33,32,30,27,26,24,23])

    >>> set_ia_turbo_ratio_limits_tpmi(trl_list=[35,33,32,30,27,26,24,23])

Inputs:
    plls         - list of PLLs. "core_<0-256>" "ring_<0-3>" (default = 'core_0')

Outputs:

EX:
    >>> histogram_dict = get_ratio_histogram()

methods.doc:
Command:
    get_ratio_avg()

Details:
    Prints the average ratio of the IA Cores/ CLR

Inputs:
    pll        - "core_<0-256>" / "ring_<0-3>" (default = 'core_0')
    interval    - interval between samples (default = 0.1[sec])
    samples     - num samples to iterate (default = 100)
    socket      - socket number (default = 0)
    return_opt  - return plls' average table (default = False)

Outputs:
    Table with summary of average/min/max ratio

EX:
    >>> get_ratio_avg()
    >>> pm_tools.gv.pstate.get_ratio_avg(pll="core_5",samples=10, socket=0)

methods.name: pysv_mod_to_pcode_vars_mod_inst
methods.doc:
This functions maps the enabled modules in PythonSV to match internal Punit regs (pcode.vars) 
associated to each module.

For example, if we dont have compute2 in a CBB, all modules instances are not going to match the internal pcode.vars.
All compute3 modules instances are shifted and the pcode.vars instances will be equal to PyhtonSV instance - 8.
This 8 should be multiplied by the computes missing before compute3 in this example.
Example output table:
+--------------------------------+--------------------------+-------------------------+
|            Modules             | Pcode instance (per CBB) | PySV instance (per CBB) |
+--------------------------------+--------------------------+-------------------------+
| socket0.cbb0.compute0.module0  |            0             |            0            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute0.module1  |            1             |            1            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute0.module2  |            2             |            2            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute0.module3  |            3             |            3            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute0.module4  |            4             |            4            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute0.module5  |            5             |            5            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute0.module6  |            6             |            6            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute0.module7  |            7             |            7            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute1.module8  |            8             |            8            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute1.module9  |            9             |            9            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute1.module10 |            10            |           10            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute1.module11 |            11            |           11            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute1.module12 |            12            |           12            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute1.module13 |            13            |           13            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute1.module14 |            14            |           14            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute1.module15 |            15            |           15            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute3.module24 |            16            |           24            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute3.module25 |            17            |           25            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute3.module26 |            18            |           26            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute3.module27 |            19            |           27            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute3.module28 |            20            |           28            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute3.module29 |            21            |           29            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute3.module30 |            22            |           30            |
| socket0.cbb0.compute3.module31 |            23            |           31            |
+--------------------------------+--------------------------+-------------------------+
Args:
    to_print: bool, to print the table of module mapping in the console. All
    modules are printed, even the ones without cores.
Return:
    mapping_dict: dict of dicts, with module path as keys, each dict contains another
    dict with both PythonSV instance (key is 'pysv_inst') and Pcode vars 
    instance (key is 'pcode_inst').

methods.name: get_sst_status
methods.doc:
Description:
    Retrieves and prints the current status of Speed Select Technology (SST) for each die in the system. The status includes the current Performance Profile (PP) level, Turbo Frequency (TF) status, Base Frequency (BF) status, Core Power (CP) status, and CP type.
Args:
    return_dict (bool): If True, the function returns a list of dictionaries containing the SST status for each die. If False, it prints the status in a table format. Default is False.
Returns:
    If return_dict is True, returns a list of dictionaries with SST status for each die. If return_dict is False, returns 0 after printing the status.

methods.name: get_core_vf_curves
methods.doc:
Command:
    get_core_vf_curves(core=None, return_option=False)

Details:
    For a given pySV core node, reads all VF curve fuse points
    (base, cdyn deltas per curve index, aging delta, MLC) and
    prints them as a table.
    Voltage encodings follow DMR CBB fuse spec:
        base voltage  : U1.8
        cdyn / aging  : U-2.8
        MLC voltage   : U1.8 (packed 9-bit fields in acode_spare_words)
    Ratio units: 100 MHz steps.

Inputs:
    core          - pySV core node, e.g. sv.socket0.cbb0.compute0.module0.core0
                    (default = None: prints the first core in sv.sockets.cbbs.computes.modules.cores)
    return_option - return (headers, rows) tuple (default = False)

Outputs:
    Prints VF curve table for the core.
    If return_option=True returns (headers, rows).

EX:
    >>> ps = pstate()
    >>> ps.get_core_vf_curves()                                          # first core
    >>> ps.get_core_vf_curves(sv.socket0.cbb0.compute0.module0.core0)
    >>> ps.get_core_vf_curves(sv.socket0.cbb0.compute0.module0.core0, return_option=True)

methods.name: get_all_cores_vf_points
methods.doc:
Command:
    get_all_cores_vf_points(return_option=False)

Details:
    For every module in the system reads the current operating ratio
    and ICCP license index, then looks up the corresponding voltage
    from each VF curve (base, cdyn at the ICCP-assigned index, aging).
    Prints a summary table with expected VF total vs actual voltage.
    NOTE: halt the system before calling to get stable register values.

Inputs:
    return_option - return (headers, rows) tuple (default = False)

Outputs:
    Prints table of current VF operating points for all cores.
    If return_option=True returns (headers, rows).

EX:
    >>> ps = pstate()
    >>> ps.get_all_cores_vf_points()
    >>> ps.get_all_cores_vf_points(return_option=True)

Team:  PostDNV PM

email: emiliano.gomez.guerrero@intel.com 
Date:  Mar 2024 

Intent of the script: Contains several utilities related to resets. For debug or test purposes (not for standalone usage).
                      Use it on a host to reboot SUT.

Input arguments/options for the script (most used by scripts): 
    - N/A

Script usage exaple: 
    For debug or test purposes (not for standalone usage).

functions.name: cold_reset_uefi
functions.doc:
Description:
    The script will write in the thread port 0xCF9 a 0xE which will trigger a cold reset
----------
Input Parameters
    - after_trigger_timeout: This is the timeout desired after the write is done in the port. 
    Default = 60s.
    - retries: Number of retries after timeout for register reading, which is :
        + sv.sockets[0].uncore.punit.bios_reset_cpl_cfg.rst_cpl4
    Default = 10 retries.
    - retry_delay: Delay for each retry iteration.
    Default = 20s.
    - general_delays: Smaller delays between some parts of the reset execution. (Time for each delay, total delays = 3)
    Default = 15.
    - boot_timeout: Delay to reach CPL4.
    Default = 600s.
----------
Returns
    - N/A

methods.name: __init__
methods.doc:
Return a new PrettyTable instance

Arguments:

encoding - Unicode encoding scheme used to decode any encoded input
title - optional table title
field_names - list or tuple of field names
fields - list or tuple of field names to include in displays
start - index of first data row to include in output
end - index of last data row to include in output PLUS ONE (list slice style)
header - print a header showing field names (True or False)
header_style - stylisation to apply to field names in header
    ("cap", "title", "upper", "lower" or None)
border - print a border around the table (True or False)
preserve_internal_border - print a border inside the table even if
    border is disabled (True or False)
hrules - controls printing of horizontal rules after rows.
    Allowed values: HRuleStyle
vrules - controls printing of vertical rules between columns.
    Allowed values: VRuleStyle
int_format - controls formatting of integer data
float_format - controls formatting of floating point data
custom_format - controls formatting of any column using callable
min_table_width - minimum desired table width, in characters
max_table_width - maximum desired table width, in characters
min_width - minimum desired field width, in characters
max_width - maximum desired field width, in characters
padding_width - number of spaces on either side of column data
    (only used if left and right paddings are None)
left_padding_width - number of spaces on left hand side of column data
right_padding_width - number of spaces on right hand side of column data
vertical_char - single character string used to draw vertical lines
horizontal_char - single character string used to draw horizontal lines
horizontal_align_char - single character string used to indicate alignment
junction_char - single character string used to draw line junctions
top_junction_char - single character string used to draw top line junctions
bottom_junction_char -
    single character string used to draw bottom line junctions
right_junction_char - single character string used to draw right line junctions
left_junction_char - single character string used to draw left line junctions
top_right_junction_char -
    single character string used to draw top-right line junctions
top_left_junction_char -
    single character string used to draw top-left line junctions
bottom_right_junction_char -
    single character string used to draw bottom-right line junctions
bottom_left_junction_char -
    single character string used to draw bottom-left line junctions
sortby - name of field to sort rows by
sort_key - sorting key function, applied to data points before sorting
align - default align for each column (None, "l", "c" or "r")
valign - default valign for each row (None, "t", "m" or "b")
reversesort - True or False to sort in descending or ascending order
oldsortslice - Slice rows before sorting in the "old style"

Add rows to the table

Add a row to the table

methods.doc:
Delete a row from the table

Arguments:

row_index - The index of the row you want to delete.  Indexing starts at 0.

methods.name: add_column
methods.doc:
Add a column to the table.

Arguments:

fieldname - name of the field to contain the new column of data
column - column of data, should be a list with as many elements as the
table has rows
align - desired alignment for this column - "l" for left, "c" for centre and
    "r" for right
valign - desired vertical alignment for new columns - "t" for top,
    "m" for middle and "b" for bottom

methods.doc: Delete all rows from the table but keep the current field names

methods.doc:
Delete all rows and field names from the table, maintaining nothing but
styling options

methods.name: get_string
methods.doc:
Return string representation of table in current state.

Arguments:

title - optional table title
start - index of first data row to include in output
end - index of last data row to include in output PLUS ONE (list slice style)
fields - names of fields (columns) to include
header - print a header showing field names (True or False)
border - print a border around the table (True or False)
preserve_internal_border - print a border inside the table even if
    border is disabled (True or False)
hrules - controls printing of horizontal rules after rows.
    Allowed values: HRuleStyle
vrules - controls printing of vertical rules between columns.
    Allowed values: VRuleStyle
int_format - controls formatting of integer data
float_format - controls formatting of floating point data
custom_format - controls formatting of any column using callable
padding_width - number of spaces on either side of column data (only used if
    left and right paddings are None)
left_padding_width - number of spaces on left hand side of column data
right_padding_width - number of spaces on right hand side of column data
vertical_char - single character string used to draw vertical lines
horizontal_char - single character string used to draw horizontal lines
horizontal_align_char - single character string used to indicate alignment
junction_char - single character string used to draw line junctions
junction_char - single character string used to draw line junctions
top_junction_char - single character string used to draw top line junctions
bottom_junction_char -
    single character string used to draw bottom line junctions
right_junction_char - single character string used to draw right line junctions
left_junction_char - single character string used to draw left line junctions
top_right_junction_char -
    single character string used to draw top-right line junctions
top_left_junction_char -
    single character string used to draw top-left line junctions
bottom_right_junction_char -
    single character string used to draw bottom-right line junctions
bottom_left_junction_char -
    single character string used to draw bottom-left line junctions
sortby - name of field to sort rows by
sort_key - sorting key function, applied to data points before sorting
reversesort - True or False to sort in descending or ascending order
print empty - if True, stringify just the header for an empty table,
    if False return an empty string

methods.name: get_csv_string
methods.doc:
Return string representation of CSV formatted table in the current state

Keyword arguments are first interpreted as table formatting options, and
then any unused keyword arguments are passed to csv.writer(). For
example, get_csv_string(header=False, delimiter='       ') would use
header as a PrettyTable formatting option (skip the header row) and
delimiter as a csv.writer keyword argument.

methods.name: get_json_string
methods.doc:
Return string representation of JSON formatted table in the current state

Keyword arguments are first interpreted as table formatting options, and
then any unused keyword arguments are passed to json.dumps(). For
example, get_json_string(header=False, indent=2) would use header as
a PrettyTable formatting option (skip the header row) and indent as a
json.dumps keyword argument.

methods.name: get_html_string
methods.doc:
Return string representation of HTML formatted version of table in current state. Arguments: title - optional table title start - index of first data row to include in output end - index of last data row to include in output PLUS ONE (list slice style) fields - names of fields (columns) to include header - print a header showing field names (True or False) escape_header - escapes the text within a header (True or False) border - print a border around the table (True or False) preserve_internal_border - print a border inside the table even if border is disabled (True or False) hrules - controls printing of horizontal rules after rows. Allowed values: HRuleStyle vrules - controls printing of vertical rules between columns. Allowed values: VRuleStyle int_format - controls formatting of integer data float_format - controls formatting of floating point data custom_format - controls formatting of any column using callable padding_width - number of spaces on either side of column data (only used if left and right paddings are None) left_padding_width - number of spaces on left hand side of column data right_padding_width - number of spaces on right hand side of column data sortby - name of field to sort rows by sort_key - sorting key function, applied to data points before sorting attributes - dictionary of name/value pairs to include as HTML attributes in the tag format - Controls whether or not HTML tables are formatted to match styling options (True or False) escape_data - escapes the text within a data field (True or False) xhtml - print
tags if True,
tags if False

methods.name: plr_mailbox
methods.doc:
Mailbox to get the Performance Limit Reasons.
https://docs.intel.com/documents/pm_doc/src/server/DMR/PM%20Features/DMR_PLR.html
args: 
    domain: 0 for core, 1 for ring. Default is 0. For domain 1, output (dict) will be PLR for all CCfs.
    module_obj: Module object. Use this option for better mapping. Default is None.
    print_msg: Boolean to print messages. Default is False.
    log: Logger object. Default is _log.
return:
    int | dict

methods.name: plr_decode
methods.doc:
This function will decode the Performance Limit Reasons bits.
DMR PLR: https://docs.intel.com/documents/pm_doc/src/server/DMR/PM%20Features/DMR_PLR.html
args: 
    plr_data: Data from the PLR mailbox and to be decoded (64 bits)
return:
    plr_coarse_status: List with the Die Level Coarse grained Perf limit reasons bits.
    plr_coarse_log: List with the logged (clear by SW) Die Level Coarse grained Perf limit reasons bits.
    plr_fine_level:n List with the Fine grained Perf limit reasons bits.

methods.name: clear_plr_mailbox
methods.doc:
Clear the PLR mailbox. If domain arg is 0 it will clear all core PLR mailboxes. If domain is 1 it will clear the CCF PLR mailbox.
args: 
    domain: 0 for core, 1 for ring. Default is 0.
return: 
    None

methods.name: get_all_edp_calcs
methods.doc:
Description: 
ia_Pwr = Cdyn * V * V * Freq + (leakage pwer) # per core
ring_Pwr = Cdyn * V * V * Freq + (leakage pwer)
fivr loss shouldn't be more than 20%
shouldn't really see more than 1-2 watts here


Cdyn should be around 5nF-6nF from the below equation
# use this for avx cdyn # Correction of the Cdyn in the voltage. Cdyn=(V-Vref)*Cdyn_max*correction+Cdyn_max
# multiplier should be ar
# use ia_fma_pwr tables for this too

        the edp pwr now has 3 tables. base_ptr = &EDP_IA_RESOLVED_ICCP_PWR_TABLE_PTR + AVX table offset if applicable

methods.name: sweep_edp_calcs
methods.doc:
Description: 
# setup initial
----------------

# loop over 10 points of iccmax
for iccmax in range(min_iccmax,iccmax+1,(iccmax-min_iccmax/10)):
    #sweep uncore frequencies
    for uRatio in range(uMeshCutOffRAtio,uMaxRatio+1):
        for lip in droop_fsm_lips:
            # from the .dbg file
            $_CFC_ISSUE_WP_WRAPPER_ADD_VBUMP 32e93h
            $_CFC_ISSUE_WP_WRAPPER_OFF 32e6ah
            $_CFC_ISSUE_WP_WRAPPER_SEND_WORKPOINT 32edeh
            $_CFC_ISSUE_WP_WRAPPER_SUB_VBUMP 32f01h
            $_CFC_ISSUE_WP_WRAPPER_WAIT_ADD_VBUMP 32ed2h
            $_CFC_ISSUE_WP_WRAPPER_WAIT_SUB_VBUMP 32f18h
            $_CFC_ISSUE_WP_WRAPPER_WAIT_WORKPOINT 32ef5h

email: vishnu.challa@intel.com 

Intent of the script: Script contains various functions which serve as the building blocks for focus & pmx tests for RACL 

Script usage example:
    Usage as standalone script or a call from another file:
        Eg: import diamondrapids.pm.pmutils.racl as racl
           racl.

functions.name: access_pcodevars
functions.doc:
Description: This function takes the pcudata register component address and composes the command to be read using u2p mb.

Params:
    register: RegisterComponent, default is None
    data: Data to write, default is None

Returns:
    int: The value read from the register or a tuple containing the success check and completion code.

functions.name: read_raw_register
functions.doc:
Description: This function takes the register component address and composes the command to be sent to patch23_raw
params:
    register: RegisterComponent, default is None
    operation: Instruction to be executed read(2)/write(3), default is 2
    reg_type: Register type, default is 9
    data: Data to write, default is None

functions.name: read_raw_vcraddress
functions.doc:
Description: This function takes the address and composes the command to be sent to patch23_raw for reads
params:

    reg_type: Register type, default is 9
    data: Data to write, default is None

Takes stored values for each field in this register and creates a
new value to write to the register.

:param skipRead: if skipRead=True, then will just assume 0 for bits

methods.name: _crmsr
methods.doc:
New CRMSR method that will take in a register or a field and perform a read if there is no data and a write if there is
Ex: _crmsr(sv.socket0.uncore.<register>, data=0xf0f0,MSR=True)
        tempReg = sv.socket0.uncore.<register>
        tempReg.storefield('<fieldname>',<fieldvalue>)
        _crmsr(tempReg,MSR=True)

Output: register/field value (hex)

methods.name: cr_package_throttle_control
methods.doc:
This function returns register for ich based throttling control, used
by tm1 sm. this is not an msr, it is accessed through mmio,
pmg_io_capture_base[15:0] - 4
when valid, this register value should be used by pcode
to override io_thread_t_req. This register is duplicated in the pcu cr space,
changes must be made in both places

methods.name: cr_package_therm_margin
methods.doc:
This function returns the register object, which contains the below fields


##############################################################################
## Register Name: pcu_cr_package_therm_margin
## ---- MultiAddress Container ----
## Current:
##      <ureg_cr available:True,offset:0x4744,size:0x4,>
## Supported:
##      (cr)=<ureg_cr available:True,offset:0x4744,size:0x4,>
##      (msr)=<msr available:True,msroffset:0x1a1,size:0x4,>
## Description:
## DTS2.0 Thermal Margin
##############################################################################
# FIELD: therm_margin
# Atribute: ro_v
# UpperBit: 15
# LowerBit: 0
# CurrentValue: 0x0L
# Description:
# Thermal margin in 8.8 format

methods.name: cr_sa_temperature_debug_field
methods.doc:
Use this function to set or read the dfx temperatures for Uncore sensors

This function will return temperature field object for the defined uncore sensor dfx hook.
The indices will vary depending on whether we are on JKT/IVT or HSX

HSX::
    SA_id=0 - return global SA temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_5 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP__SAFUSEHSX.dts0(fusethermdiode0)[FUSE]
    SA_id=1 - return global CGU sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_0 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_0.dts0(qiothrmdiode)[QIO]
    SA_id=2 - return global CGU sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_6 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_0.dts1(thermdiode0)[CGU]
    SA_id=3 - return global HA0 sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_2 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_2.dts0(hatherm_diode)[HAMC0]
    SA_id=4 - return global HA0 sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_8 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_2.dts1(ddrtherm_dio)[DDR01]
    SA_id=5 - return global HA1 sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_3 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_3.dts0(hatherm_diode)[HAMC1]
    SA_id=6 - return global HA1 sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_9 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_3.dts1(ddrthermdio)[DDR23]
    SA_id=7 - return global PIO_NE_GAP sensor0 temperature <- global_sa_unit_temp_1 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_1.dts0(piothrmdiode)[PIO]
    SA_id=8 - return global PIO_NE_GAP sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_7 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_1.dts1(thermdiode_rcn)[RCN]
    SA_id=9 -  return global COLD_NE_RCN sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_4 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_4.dts0(thermdiode_me)[COLD]
    SA_id=10 - return global COLD_NE_RCN sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_10 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_4.dts1(qio2thrmdiode)[QIO2]
    ---register_mapping={0:5,1:0,2:6,3:2,4:8,5:3,6:9,7:1,8:7,9:4,10:10} #SA_id to register mapping

ref_temp -  #This field indicates the maximum junction temperature, also referred to as
            # the Throttle Temperature, TCC Activation Temperature or Prochot Temperature.
            # This is the temperature at which the Thermal Monitor is activated. FW updates
            # this register to the following

fan_temp_target_ofst

methods.name: msr_therm_status
methods.doc:
Returns core scoped therm_status register

therm_status should be package scope (must be getting duplicated to each core)

valid (31:31) -- This bit indicates that the TEMPERATURE field is valid
resolution (30:27) -- Supported resolution in degrees C
temperature (22:16) -- Digital temperature reading in degreesC relative to the factory configured Tcc activation temperature
power_limitation_log (11:11) -- Sticky bit that indicates whether the CPU was forced to reduce P states below OS requested P ...
power_limitation_status (10:10) -- Indicates whether the CPU was forced to reduce P states below OS requested P State (via IA...
threshold2_log (09:09) -- Sticky bit that indicates whether the Thermal Threshold 2 has been reached since the last clearing ...
threshold2_status (08:08) -- Indicates that the current temperature is higher than or equal to Threshold 2 temperature
threshold1_log (07:07) -- Sticky bit that indicates whether the Thermal Threshold #1 has been reached since the last clearing...
threshold1_status (06:06) -- Indicates that the current temperature is higher than or equal to Threshold 1 temperature
out_of_spec_log (05:05) -- When set, this bit indicates that the temperature has been above the maximum operating temperature...
out_of_spec_status (04:04) -- When set, this bit indicates whether the temperature is currently above the maximum operating t...
prochot_log (03:03) -- Sticky bit that indicates whether a catastrophic cooling failure occurs as indicated by assertion of t...
prochot_status (02:02) -- Indicates whether PROCHOT# signal is being asserted                 <--External prochot
thermal_monitor_log (01:01) -- This is a sticky bit that indicates the history of the thermal sensor high temperature output ...
thermal_monitor_status (00:00) -- This bit indicates whether the thermal sensor high-temperature output signal (PROCHOT#) is ...    <---Internal Prochot

methods.name: pcu_io_temp_unpub_core
methods.doc:
This Function returns calibrated (Unpublished) core temperature values from pcode:::

Calibrated temperature values are stored in the following pcudata registers:
For core sensors:
pcudata.thermal_unpublished_ia_sensor[0,1]_temp_[0-17]
For uncore sensors:
pcudata.thermal_unpublished_sa_temp_[0-10]

Format is s16.8.8 and can be converted to float by:
t._bin2float(int(r.thermal.pcu_io_temp_unpub_core()),'s16.8.8')

methods.name: pcu_io_temp_unpub_sa
methods.doc:
This Function returns calibrated (Unpublished) sa temperature values from pcode:::

Calibrated temperature values are stored in the following pcudata registers:
For core sensors:
pcudata.thermal_unpublished_ia_sensor[0,1]_temp_[0-17]
For uncore sensors:
pcudata.thermal_unpublished_sa_temp_[0-10]

Format is s16.8.8 and can be converted to float by:
t._bin2float(int(r.thermal.pcu_io_temp_unpub_core()),'s16.8.8')

HSX::
    SA_id=0 - return global SA temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_5 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_SAFUSEHSX.dts0(fusethermdiode0)[FUSE]
    SA_id=1 - return global CGU sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_0 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_0.dts0(qiothrmdiode)[QIO]
    SA_id=2 - return global CGU sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_6 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_0.dts1(thermdiode0)[CGU]
    SA_id=3 - return global HA0 sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_2 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_2.dts0(hatherm_diode)[HAMC0]
    SA_id=4 - return global HA0 sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_8 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_2.dts1(ddrtherm_dio)[DDR01]
    SA_id=5 - return global HA1 sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_3 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_3.dts0(hatherm_diode)[HAMC1]
    SA_id=6 - return global HA1 sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_9 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_3.dts1(ddrthermdio)[DDR23]
    SA_id=7 - return global PIO_NE_GAP sensor0 temperature <- global_sa_unit_temp_1 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_1.dts0(piothrmdiode)[PIO]
    SA_id=8 - return global PIO_NE_GAP sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_7 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_1.dts1(thermdiode_rcn)[RCN]
    SA_id=9 -  return global COLD_NE_RCN sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_4 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_4.dts0(thermdiode_me)[COLD]
    SA_id=10 - return global COLD_NE_RCN sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_10 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_4.dts1(qio2thrmdiode)[QIO2]

    register_mapping={0:5,1:0,2:6,3:2,4:8,5:3,6:9,7:1,8:7,9:4,10:10}

methods.name: pcu_io_core_status
methods.doc:
Description: per-core status register. lower bits can be written by microcode, while higher bits are internal pcu signals. for pcode, it is completely read-only. this register is duplicated in the pcu cr space, changes must be made in both places core_active (31:31) -- virtual signal from the rob, signaling that the core is active wakeup_request (30:30) -- virtual signal from the ncu, specifying that there is an event pending
for the core core_in_c3_c6 (28:28) -- the state of the core determined at dispatcher completion, 0 means that it
is cc0, ... probe_mode_done (27:27) -- core indication to release probe mode state disable_wakeup_req (26:26) --
this bit is used only by gt along with the pm-block-req bit s1_ack (25:25) --
this bit is set by an ups virtual signal from the pma when the core has acknowledged the ... pm_block_req (24:24) --
microcode/gt writeable field rc6_entry (23:23) --
this field only has meaning for the gt register instance cold_reset_not_complete (21:21) --
was rc6_entry on jkt/snb core_wish_sub_state (15:12) --
microcode/gt writeable field, specifying the coordinated wish sub cc-state core_wish_state (11:08) -- C0 --
microcode/gt writeable field, specifying the coordinated wish cc-state core_cpd_state (06:04) -- Normal --
microcode/gt writeable field, specifying the cpd (core power down) state core_state (02:00) -- C0 --
microcode/gt writeable field, specifying the cc-state where microcode is

methods.name: pcu_io_thread_t_req_thread
methods.doc:
## Register Name: io_thread_t_req_thread0
## Type         : pcuram
## Offset      : 0xc200
## Bits Offset  : 0
##
## Description:
##        thread t-state value, which is taken from msr-19a
## (ia32_clock_modulation).       this register is duplicated in the pcu cr
## space, changes must be made in both places

# FIELD: throttle_active
# Atribute: read_only
# UpperBit: 4
# LowerBit: 4
# CurrentValue: 0x0L
# Description:
# if set, we are in t-state throttle mode

# FIELD: t_state_req
# Atribute: read_only
# UpperBit: 3
# LowerBit: 0
# CurrentValue: 0x0L
# Description:
# duty cycle value. a value of xx means that the 'on' time is xx/16.
#         a value of 0 means 50% d.c. maximal value is 15/16. to disable
# throttle, use the throttle_active bit.

methods.name: pcu_io_package_throttle_control
methods.doc:
This function returns register for ich based throttling control, used
by tm1 sm. this is not an msr, it is accessed through mmio,
pmg_io_capture_base[15:0] - 4
when valid, this register value should be used by pcode
to override io_thread_t_req. This register is duplicated in the pcu cr space,
changes must be made in both places

methods.name: pcu_cr_thread_t_req_thread
methods.doc:
Returns the thead scope register object

## Register Name: pcu_cr_thread_t_req
## ---- MultiAddress Container ----
## Current:
##      <regbus bitsoffset:0x0,offset:0x46b8,size:0x4,>
## Supported:
##      (cr)=<regbus bitsoffset:0x0,offset:0x46b8,size:0x4,>
##      (msr)=<msr available:True,msroffset:0x19a,size:0x4,>
## Description:
## This register is used to read and write the duty cycle bits, and to enable
## the on-demand clock modulation feature of the Intel Thermal Monitor.  The
## actual duty cycle of the core having two threads will be the maximum duty
## cycle of both the threads in an SMT configuration.
## This function is non-functional for ICX

methods.name: pcu_data_global_temp_sa
methods.doc:
This function returns calibrated global SA temps from the PCU and is
backwards compatible with JKT and IVT. Below are
the behaviors for various SA_ids:

methods.name: pcu_fused_sa_therm_sensor_cal_slope
methods.doc:
JKT/IVT::
    SA_id=0 - return global SA temperature
    SA_id=1 - return global SA south temperature
    SA_id=2 - return global SA South East temperature

HSX::
    SA_id=0 - return global SA temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_5 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP__SAFUSEHSX.dts0(fusethermdiode0)[FUSE]
    SA_id=1 - return global CGU sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_0 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_0.dts0(qiothrmdiode)[QIO]
    SA_id=2 - return global CGU sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_6 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_0.dts1(thermdiode0)[CGU]
    SA_id=3 - return global HA0 sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_2 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_2.dts0(hatherm_diode)[HAMC0]
    SA_id=4 - return global HA0 sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_8 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_2.dts1(ddrtherm_dio)[DDR01]
    SA_id=5 - return global HA1 sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_3 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_3.dts0(hatherm_diode)[HAMC1]
    SA_id=6 - return global HA1 sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_9 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_3.dts1(ddrthermdio)[DDR23]
    SA_id=7 - return global PIO_NE_GAP sensor0 temperature <- global_sa_unit_temp_1 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_1.dts0(piothrmdiode)[PIO]
    SA_id=8 - return global PIO_NE_GAP sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_7 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_1.dts1(thermdiode_rcn)[RCN]
    SA_id=9 -  return global COLD_NE_RCN sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_4 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_4.dts0(thermdiode_me)[COLD]
    SA_id=10 - return global COLD_NE_RCN sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_10 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_4.dts1(qio2thrmdiode)[QIO2]
    ---register_mapping={0:5,1:0,2:6,3:2,4:8,5:3,6:9,7:1,8:7,9:4,10:10} #SA_id to register mapping

methods.name: pcu_fused_sa_therm_sensor_cal_hot_val
methods.doc:
JKT/IVT::
    SA_id=0 - return global SA temperature
    SA_id=1 - return global SA south temperature

HSX::
    SA_id=0 - return global SA temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_5 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP__SAFUSEHSX.dts0(fusethermdiode0)[FUSE]

    SA_id=3 - return global HA0 sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_2 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_2.dts0(hatherm_diode)[HAMC0]
    SA_id=4 - return global HA0 sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_8 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_2.dts1(ddrtherm_dio)[DDR01]
    SA_id=5 - return global HA1 sensor0 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_3 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_3.dts0(hatherm_diode)[HAMC1]
    SA_id=6 - return global HA1 sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_9 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_3.dts1(ddrthermdio)[DDR23]
    SA_id=7 - return global PIO_NE_GAP sensor0 temperature <- global_sa_unit_temp_1 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_1.dts0(piothrmdiode)[PIO]
    SA_id=8 - return global PIO_NE_GAP sensor1 temperature <-global_sa_unit_temp_7 <-IO_UNCAL_TEMP_UNCORE_1.dts1(thermdiode_rcn)[RCN]

methods.name: pcu_data_tm1_configuration_state
methods.doc:
returns tm1_configuration_state_# which corresponds to &TM1_CONFIGURATION_STATE
in the pcuram. &TM1_CONFIGURATION_STATE keeps the last tm1
request that went over pmlink dns


##############################################################################
## Register Name: io_tt1fsm_control_core0
## Type         : pcuram
## Offset      : 0xf540
## Bits Offset  : 0
##
## Description:
## pcode control of the tt1 fsm.
##############################################################################
# FIELD: throttle_enable
# Atribute: read_write
# UpperBit: 31
# LowerBit: 31
# CurrentValue: 0x0L
# Description:
# If set, we are in TT1 throttle mode
##############################################################################
# FIELD: off_time
# Atribute: read_write
# UpperBit: 15
# LowerBit: 8
# CurrentValue: 0x0L
# Description:
# TT1 clock off time. in 160ns granularity.
##############################################################################
# FIELD: on_time
# Atribute: read_write
# UpperBit: 7
# LowerBit: 0
# CurrentValue: 0x0L
# Description:
# TT1 clock on time. in 160ns granularity.

tm1_configuration_state_<core#> should have the same bit mapping
Duty cycle can be calculated as: on_time/(on_time+off_time)

methods.name: pcu_cr_therm_event_ffm
methods.doc: This register is configure ffm mode and see if SMI signaled

methods.name: ffm_smi_signaled
methods.doc:
# FIELD: ffm_smi_signaled
# Atribute: rw0c
# UpperBit: 1
# LowerBit: 1
# CurrentValue: 0x0L
# Description:
# BIOS in SMM mode is responsible for checking this bit to determine if the origin of the SMI is from a thermal event or not.  When set, the interrupt is thermal related and BIOS must read the
# thermal status registers to determine which cores to send an IPI to.  PCU FW will read this bit before sending a thermal related SMI.  If the bit is set it will not deliver another interrupt. BIOS
# must clear this bit when it is ready to receive another interrupt.

methods.name: ffm_enable_bit
methods.doc:
# FIELD: enable_ffm
# Atribute: rw
# UpperBit: 0
# LowerBit: 0
# CurrentValue: 0x0L
# Description:
# Can be set by BIOS in SMM only.  When set, instead of delivering a thermal
# interrupt to core PCU FW must send an SMI.

methods.name: zodiac_enable
methods.doc:
Enabling therm_rsvd_en bit (bit29) of pcu_cr_power_ctl1 register is how to enable zodiac.
When Zodiac is enabled, processor will perform tstates as a result of xxProchot assertion
>>? sv.socket0.uncore.punit.power_ctl1.therm_rsvd_en.write(0x1)

To search fcm_x set name = 'x', and subcomp_type='fcm'
To search pma_y set name to 'y' and subcomp_type = 'pma

methods.name: print_2D_data_table
methods.doc:
Desription: Support function to print data for multple sockets (mutiple columns).
Datalist: 2x2 [[]] list of strings, data = [[data_0],[data_1],...[data_N]]
Fieldlist: 1x1 list of strings, fields = [field_0, field_1...field_N]
Table:
                datalist_0          datalist_1    ...     datalist_M
   field_0 ->    data_0[0]           data_1[0]    ...      data_M[0]
   field_1 ->    data_0[1]           data_1[1]    ...      data_M[1]
     .              .                   .                     .
     .              .                   .                     .
     .              .                   .                     .
   field_N ->    data_0[N]           data_1[N]    ...      data_M[N]

**kwargs:
    @param: header - Data column(s) header, defaults to S0... S3.
    @param: title - Table's title.
    @param: returnTable - Boolean, use to return a formatted table for logging.
    @param: decodedict - decode dictionary for data
    @param: ljust - Boolean, Left justify data. Puts data on the left and fields on the right

methods.doc:
sample_regs is used to sample a list of registers and either just print them or save them to a csv file for later use.

Args:
    regList: The regList that can either be a list of strings or register objects. If a sublist is made you can pass a string of the converstion factor.
    sampleRate: The rate to wait between regList samples
    csvName: The name of the csvfile you want to create

Example::

    sample_regs(['socket0.pcudata.global_gvfsm_core_pstate_current_0',['socket0.pcudata.global_gvfsm_current_slice_voltage_0',"U15.2.13"]])
    sample_regs([sv.socket0.pcudata.global_gvfsm_core_pstate_current_0,[sv.socket0.pcudata.global_gvfsm_current_slice_voltage_0,"U15.2.13"]])
    sample_regs([sv.socket0.pcudata.global_gvfsm_core_pstate_current_0,[sv.socket0.pcudata.global_gvfsm_current_slice_voltage_0,"U15.2.13"]],None,"my_csv.csv")

A utility to sample a set of registers and return the distribution of values

Args:

    sockets(list,int,None): the sockets that you want to sample registers from
    samples (int): the number of register reads you would like to do
    color(bool): flag to do some pretty highlighting. If in windows you need to use the return String in your logger.
    returnString(bool): use this boolean if you want to get the raw data returned as a string.
    returnDict(bool): use this boolean if you want to get the raw data returned in a dictionary format.

module_doc:
Start/stop different workloads outside of PMX.

Sample usage:
    import pm.pmutils.workload as wl
    wl.ptat(5) # Run ptat AVX3 test
    wl.memicals() # Runs a memicals command line
    wl.mlc('mlc_internal --loaded_latency -d0 -t120 -b1k') # Runs a MLC command line
    wl.stop() # Stops all traffic generators

functions.name: ptat_cpu
functions.doc:
Kicks off a ptat test
Args:
    test: ID of the ptat test you want to run
        0: None
        1: TDP Test
        2: nTDP Test (NOTE: this test will override the core and CLM voltages)
        3: Core IA/SSE Test
        4: Core AVX2 Test
        5: Core AVX-512 Test
        6: Pmax Test
        7: Pmax.App Test
        8: Turbo Test
    power: power level for ptat test, from 50 - 100
    time : runtime for ptat test, time = 0 for non-stop run
-cpucore <bitmask>
    Specify the 64-bit core mask value for CPU tests. Default is all cores.
Returns:
    int: 0 if ptat is running, else -1

classes.name: FiniteStack
classes.doc:
This is a stack class for storing strings and outputting at a later time.  It is initialized 
with a finite length so that old items at the bottom of the stack get removed.  It can also act as 
a fo logger that the core code can write to so that all register reads and writes can be recorded.

methods.name: __init__
methods.doc: @max - the number of elements before it starts dumping the bottom of the stack

methods.name: push
pop
methods.doc:
return the top of the stack poping its value off.

WARNING: Uses locking mechanisms.  Ensure that iterrupt handlers do not call this function or deadlocks may occur.

Throws an exception if the size is not greater than zero

methods.name: newMax
methods.doc:
Change the size of the stack for whatever reason.
newMaxValue - integer value for the new stack size

methods.name: debugall
methods.doc:
This function is necessary so that we can hook all python register accesses. 
This function makes the class pose as a logger.

methods.name: dump_non_destructive
methods.doc: Dump the stack without destroying its contents. i.e. poping elements.

methods.name: dumpStackFormatted
methods.doc:
Dumps the stack formatted form.

dumpFunct - a function to output the stack to.  This must take consecutive strings and process them somehow.
            The function can be the stdout or a log file function or anything else.

lineTerminator - Placed at the end of every string written to the function,
                 Some functions may include a line termination character.  If they do leave this ''.

classes.name: _Pool
classes.doc:
A _Pool is a set of PMX2 apps that will be run in parallel. It executes the
tests, and collects their individual return values. It will also return
the sum of the their return values as the result for this set of tests.
Therefore, any non-zero result returned from 'excute' is a failure.

@return the sum of all of the results, giving a cumulative result

classes.name: _TestPool
classes.doc:
_TestPool is a subclass of _Pool. The _TestPool will attempt to start 
each application, just like _Pool. Unlike _Pool, after a specified timeout,
_TestPool will kill each app with a SIGINT (or CTRL_C_EVENT in Windows).
If the app fails to exit gracefully, a SIGTERM will be sent, and the app's
result code will be set to 1.

methods.name: __init__
methods.doc:
@param  config   the configuration for this pool of processes
@param  runtime  the timeout for the apps in this pool

methods.name: execute
methods.doc:
Execute all of the processes in the pool. After the specified timeout,
send each process a SIGINT. If they die, we are done. Otherwise, send a
SIGTERM to force them to die.
@return the sum of the return codes of the subprocesses

classes.doc: Description/Purpose:

methods.name: __init__
methods.doc: Description/Purpose: Initialize all the minimum requirements for memtrip assertion

methods.name: loopSetup
methods.doc:
Description/Purpose: Run only one time at the start of your test content. 
                     Used to store initial condition of the regs you will modify during your test
                     Test any requirements or feature enabling. e.g. Verify if the BIOS knob was correctly set, fuse requirement, etc.

functions.name: run_harasser_loop
functions.doc:
Test picks a random portids from each die and launches SB Harasser

Agrument definitions and usage:

1. pcode - disable/enable harasser

    0 - Disable Pcode harasser
    1 - Enable Pcode harasser
2. ocode - disable/enable harasser
    0 - Disable ocode harasser
    1 - Enable ocode harasser
3. acode - disable/enable PLR messaging
    0 - Disable acode harasser
    1 - Enable acode harasser    
4. pcode_operation
    0 - Read
    1 - Write
    2 - RMW
5. ocode_operation
    0-255 value programmed into bits [55:48]
6. pcode_operation_type
    0 - Non Posted
    1 - Posted
7. ocode_operation_type
    0 - Posted
    1 - Non Posted
8. pcode_access_type
    0 - CR
    1 - CFG
    2 - MEM
9. addr - default address offset is 0x0.
10. interval - time for each iteration
11. t_time - total time harasser 
12. ep_match - Parse a list of portnames.
    example: ep_match=['corepm', 'punit']
13. ip_filter - Target specific IP blocks by name (case-insensitive substring match)
    example: ip_filter=['punit', 'corepm', 'ubox']
14. ip_filter_out - Skip specific IP blocks by name (case-insensitive substring match)
    example: ip_filter_out=['dummy', 'ubox']

Examples: 
    # Basic usage
    run_harasser_loop(interval=30, t_time=600) # runs for 600sec and for every 30sec picks random portids
    
    # IP-specific targeting
    run_harasser_loop(interval=30, t_time=600, ip_filter=['punit', 'corepm'])  # Only targets punit and corepm IPs
    run_harasser_loop(interval=30, t_time=600, ip_filter=['ubox'], pcode=1, ocode=0, acode=0)  # Only pcode on ubox

module_doc:
trackers.py

This module provides utilities for locating and processing tracker-related information
from an 'emurun.exe' file within a project directory. It includes functions to:

- Search for the 'emurun.exe' file in a directory tree.
- Extract the workarea path from the 'emurun.exe' file.
- Identify enabled trackers by parsing the file.
- Execute post-processing scripts for detected trackers.

Typical usage involves running this script directly, which will automatically
find 'emurun.exe', extract the relevant information, and invoke the appropriate
tracker post-processing commands.

functions.name: extract_emurun_data
functions.doc:
Reads the emurun.exe file to extract both the workarea and trackers.
Handles both regular (.exe) and compressed (.gz) files.

Returns:

functions.doc: Reads fuse.log to determine if we are in MCP or IMH.

Executes tracker post-processing scripts for the specified workarea and trackers.

Args:
    work_area (str): The $WORKAREA containing the post-processing scripts.
    trackers (list of str): A list of trackers to post-process.
    mcp (bool): Whether running on MCP or IMH, required to select the correct CTH environment.
    guop_lighter (bool): Whether to enable lighter mode for GUOP trackers.

functions.name: bin2float
functions.doc:
Convert binary number into float or signed int32 format using the provided format definition

Args:
    binary (str): pass the value to be converted from a float or signed int32 format
    fmt (str): Fixed floating point format, 'float', 'double' and 'int32' (signed int32)

Format Syntax::

    S16.2.13
    ^ ^   ^
    | |   +- exponent
    | +----- significand size
    | +----- size
    +------- Signed / Unsigned

module_doc:
dvfs_bios_config.py

HSD: 16030715600 - [PSS] BIOS Configurations of DVFS settings

Verifies that BIOS has correctly configured DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency
Scaling) settings in UFS (Universal Frequency Scaling) TPMI registers across all
dies (IMH IO, IMH MEM, CBBs) after POST.

Checks performed (per die):
  - ufs_header       : interface version, fabric mask, UFS-disabled bit, fusion, ratio_unit
  - ufs_control      : throttle_mode, max_ratio, min_ratio, eff_ratio (ELC), idle_disable
  - ufs_control_fabric_1 : same fields for fabric domain 1
  - ufs_adv_control_1/2  : advanced control knobs
  - ufs_status       : current_ratio, current_voltage (sanity -- should be non-zero after BIOS)
  - opc_hwp_capability   : highest_performance == ufs max_ratio (cross-check via IMH/CBB TPMI)

BIOS knobs required:
  - ProcessorHWPMEnable = 1  (HWP enable)
  - Any DVFS knobs that modify ratio limits must be reflected in the UFS control registers
  - See setup_bios_knobs.csh for IFWI preparation steps

Key namednodes paths:
  IMH:  sv.socketX.imhY.punit.ptpcfsms.ptpcfsms.ufs_*
  CBB:  sv.socketX.cbbN.base.tpmi.ufs_*

Emulation: HSLE IMH2 M7 (MCP ICI) -- run via dvfs_bios_config_run.csh
Post-Si:   import and call main() / log_registers() from PythonSV session

functions.name: _bin2float_u3_13
functions.doc: Convert U3.13 fixed-point to float (used for UFS voltage field).

functions.name: _get_tpmi_path
functions.doc: Return the TPMI register node for a die (CBB or IMH).

module_doc:
tpmi_fabric_gv.py

HSD: 16030715607 - [PSS] TPMI-driven FabricGV

Verifies that PCode responds to TPMI OPC HWP control writes by changing the
fabric GV (Gear Voltage) ratio, observable in UFS status registers across all
dies.

Test flow:
  1. Read baseline fabric ratios from ufs_status.current_ratio and
     ufs_status_fabric_1.current_ratio on all dies.
  2. Drive a fabric frequency change request via opc_hwp_controls on IMH0:
       - Write maximum_performance = cap_hi  (request max)
       - Write minimum_performance = cap_hi  (force ratio to max)
  3. Wait for PCode to respond (emulation: wait cycles; post-si: read until stable).
  4. Read updated ratios and verify at least one die changed ratio.
  5. Drive down to minimum: write max=lo, min=lo and verify ratio decreases.
  6. Restore original controls.

Key namednodes paths:
  IMH: sv.socketX.imh0.punit.ptpcfsms.ptpcfsms.opc_hwp_controls.*
       sv.socketX.imhY.punit.ptpcfsms.ptpcfsms.ufs_status.current_ratio
       sv.socketX.imhY.punit.ptpcfsms.ptpcfsms.ufs_status_fabric_1.current_ratio
  CBB: sv.socketX.cbbN.base.tpmi.ufs_status.current_ratio
       sv.socketX.cbbN.base.tpmi.ufs_status_fabric_1.current_ratio

BIOS knobs required:
  - ProcessorHWPMEnable = 1
  - See setup_bios_knobs.csh

Emulation: HSLE IMH2 M7 (MCP ICI) -- run via tpmi_fabric_gv_run.csh
Post-Si:   import and call main() from PythonSV session

functions.name: calc_units
functions.doc:
Calculate latency value and unit encoding for PEGA timing registers.

Args:
    latencyMs (float): Latency in milliseconds
    
Returns:
    tuple: (latency_value, unit_encoding) where:
        - latency_value (int): 8-bit value for latency field (1-255)
        - unit_encoding (int): 2-bit unit encoding (0-3)
    
Unit Encodings:
    0: 640 ns (0.64 us) units
    1: 10240 ns (~10.2 us) units  
    2: 163840 ns (~164 us) units
    3: 2621440 ns (~2.6 ms) units

functions.name: decode_opc_therm_status
functions.doc:
Reads and decodes OPC_PKG_THERM_STATUS TPMI register

THERMAL_MONITOR_STATUS      0:0         1   RO/V    Status bit indicating that any of the package thermal monitor have tripped and the package is currently thermally throttling.
THERMAL_MONITOR_LOG     1:1     1   RO/V    Sticky log bit indicating that the package has seen a thermal monitor event since the last time SW cleared this bit.
PROCHOT_STATUS              2:2         1   RO/V    Status bit indicating that xxPROCHOT# is currently being asserted.
PROCHOT_LOG             3:3     1   RO/V    Sticky log bit indicating that xxPROCHOT# has been asserted since the last time this bit was cleared by SW.
OUT_OF_SPEC_STATUS      4:4     1   RO/V    Status bit indicating that the processor is operating out of its thermal specification.
OUT_OF_SPEC_LOG             5:5         1   RO/V    Sticky log bit indicating that the processor operating out of its thermal specification since the last time this bit was cleared.
THRESHOLD1_STATUS       6:6     1   RO/V    Indicates that the current temperature is higher than or equal to the Threshold1 defined in the IA32_PACKAGE_THERM_INTERRUPT MSR.
THRESHOLD1_LOG              7:7         1   RO/V    Sticky log bit that asserts on a 0 to 1 or 1 to 0 transition of the Threshold1_Status bit.
THRESHOLD2_STATUS       8:8     1   RO/V    Indicates that the current temperature is higher than or equal to the Threshold2 defined in the IA32_PACKAGE_THERM_INTERRUPT MSR.
THRESHOLD2_LOG              9:9         1   RO/V    Sticky log bit that asserts on a 0 to 1 or 1 to 0 transition of the Threshold2_Status bit.
POWER_LIMITATION_STATUS     10:10   1       RO/V    Indicates whether the current P-state is limited by power limitation.
POWER_LIMITATION_LOG        11:11   1       RO/V    Sticky log bit that indicates whether the current P-state is limited by power limitation since the last clearing of this bit or a reset.
PMAX_STATUS             12:12       1       RO/V    Status bit for the PMAX detector. An assertion means the PMAX detector circuit has asserted (up to a 1ms delay). The status bit is deasserted when the core throttle is released to indicate the PMAX condition has been dealt with.
PMAX_LOG                13:13       1       RO/V    Sticky log bit indicating the PMAX detector has been asserted since the last time this bit was cleared by SW.
RSVD                        63:14   50      RO/V    Reserved.       0

functions.name: main
functions.doc: Main function to execute PLR mailbox command and decode the results.
